one_line_takeaway: SCP is a protocol-design proposal (formal message envelope, five-phase audit-blocking lifecycle, five-class DRM-style threat model, and a log-proportional revenue formula) for attributing and licensing human-generated content consumed by LLMs, backed only by a small unvalidated reference implementation and preliminary sub-100ms latency benchmarks on a ~120-item synthetic dataset -- it is a systems/policy-infrastructure paper, not an empirical attack/defense study.
key_finding: The paper's only quantitative results are preliminary latency benchmarks (Table III, n=100 per method) on a synthetic dataset of 10 creators with 8-15 content items each (~120 content pieces total) across five verticals (travel, food, technology, journalism, fashion): getCreatorProfile P50/P95/P99 = 19/22/23 ms; searchCreators = 29/45/45 ms; getCreatorContent (structured) = 22/24/30 ms; getCreatorContent (semantic) = 45/66/69 ms; getCreatorScore = 20/36/41 ms; verifyAuthenticity = 14/20/35 ms; getAccessLog = 14/24/38 ms. Semantic-search methods are the slowest (dominated by vector-store query and embedding time), and the paper's own headline claim is that 'all methods remain well under 100ms at P99, establishing that SCP adds minimal overhead for real-time inference use cases.' The paper caveats that the reference implementation uses a lightweight hash-based embedding function for portability, so a production deployment using a real neural embedding model (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2, which is named as the intended production model but was apparently not what was actually benchmarked) 'would see higher latencies on semantic search methods' -- i.e., the reported numbers likely understate real-world latency.
core_contribution: The paper proposes the Sovereign Context Protocol (SCP), an open-source protocol specification and reference architecture that sits as an attribution-aware data access layer between LLMs and human-generated content, positioned as analogous to Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP) but for creator-owned data instead of tools. SCP defines six methods (getCreatorProfile, searchCreators, getCreatorContent, getCreatorScore, verifyAuthenticity, getAccessLog) exposed over REST and MCP-compatible interfaces, wraps every response in a Response Envelope binding data + attribution + a license envelope (SHA-256 content fingerprint, usage terms, revocation status) + an audit_log_id, and enforces a five-phase lifecycle (authenticate -> execute -> license generation -> synchronous/blocking audit log write -> respond) in which a failed audit write fails the whole request ('no data is served without attribution'). The paper's own contributions, as it explicitly enumerates them (Sec I and Sec VII-B), are: (1) the formal protocol spec and message envelope; (2) a threat model with five adversary classes; (3) a log-proportional revenue attribution model; (4) a reference implementation (~2,500 LOC, Apache 2.0, FastAPI + SQLite + ChromaDB + NetworkX) with preliminary latency benchmarks; (5) a mapping of SCP methods to MCP tool/resource primitives; and (6) a discussion of SCP's relationship to the EU AI Act Article 53, C2PA, and the broader regulatory landscape. The paper is explicit (Sec VII-B, 'What is Novel, What is Not') that the dual-store (knowledge graph + vector DB) retrieval architecture, API-key auth, audit logging, and license management are individually pre-existing/mature techniques; its claimed novelty is the specific combination and protocol-level framing for the LLM-creator attribution problem, not any of the individual components.
threat_model: Section IV defines five adversary classes against the SCP protocol itself (not against an underlying ML model): T1 Retention beyond license - a consumer caches served data and reuses it after license expiry/revocation; mitigated only by detectability (audit log + content fingerprint enable forensic/contractual after-the-fact action), explicitly analogized to DRM ('the value is in making non-compliance costly, not technically impossible'). T2 Sybil consumers - an entity creates multiple consumer accounts to evade rate limits/aggregate data beyond intended scope; mitigation is organizational identity verification and anomaly detection over the audit log. T3 Reconstruction attacks - many small targeted queries used to reconstruct a creator's full corpus; mitigation is query-pattern monitoring, per-creator/per-consumer access budgets, and aggregation alerts. T4 Consumer collusion - multiple consumers pool SCP-served data to bypass per-consumer licensing; mitigation relies on consumer-specific fingerprints making shared data traceable, not preventable. T5 Adversarial score manipulation - a creator inflates engagement signals to raise their heuristic Value/Trust Score; mitigation is multi-signal validation and (unimplemented) ML-based anomaly detection. The paper is explicit that this is a protocol-compliance/DRM-style threat model (detect-and-attribute, not prevent), not a threat model for adversarial attacks on an ML model's training or inference.
limitations: The paper self-reports five limitations in Sec VII-E: L1 post-access enforcement is purely contractual/detective (SCP 'cannot prevent a determined bad actor from retaining data'); L2 the protocol's value is adoption-dependent ('a protocol with ten creators is a curiosity; ten million is infrastructure') and the bootstrapping/chicken-and-egg adoption problem is acknowledged but not solved; L3 the Value/Trust scoring is an unvalidated ad-hoc heuristic formula requiring future empirical calibration and adversarial testing; L4 the protocol is text-only in v1.0, with image/audio/video extension described as architecturally straightforward but unimplemented; L5 the latency benchmarks are preliminary, on a small synthetic dataset (~120 items, 10 creators), single-core/4GB RAM, with scaling behavior under millions of items and concurrent consumers left as open future work. Beyond the paper's own list: none of the five threat-model mitigations (T1-T5) are actually implemented or tested in the reference system -- they are proposed design directions ('mitigation: X should...' / 'can be applied'); the revenue-attribution model's method weights lambda(m) are left unspecified/ungoverned rather than derived; and there is no legal analysis validating that the protocol's 'consent revocation' has any enforceability against data already baked into trained model weights, a point the paper itself flags as an open legal question it does not resolve.

datasets_used: Synthetic dataset of 10 creators with 8-15 content items each (~120 total content pieces) across five verticals: travel, food, technology, journalism, fashion (used only for the latency benchmarks in Table III)
quantitative_results: Reference implementation is ~2,500 lines of code (Apache 2.0 license)
Benchmarks run with n=100 requests per method on a single-core Linux instance with 4GB RAM
